taskVersion.name: epu_q2_medium
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_medium")
def epu_q2_medium(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, medium_pre, medium_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

TARGET ARTICLE START
New Data Point to Weakening is.S. Economy: Drop in 3 Measures Prompts ... By ROBERT D. HERSHEY Jr.Special to The New York Times New York Times (1923-Current file); Jun 30, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. D1 New Data Point to Weakening is.S. Economy Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, June 29 — In a three-pronged indication of a weaken­ing economy, fresh reports today showed a plunge in new-home sales and unsettling, albeit moderate, de­clines in consumer confidence and the Government's chief forecasting index. Although most analysts continued to expect growth for the second half of the year to exceed that of the very sluggish first half, today's data prompted expressions of concern that the risks to further expansion had grown. 'Economy Could Turn Down' "The economy could turn down — or it could slow down" some more, said Larry Moran, a Commerce De­partment economist. He referred spe­cifically to the three-tenths of 1 per­cent decline in the department's in­dex of leading indicators for May, the fifth straight month in which the in­dex reversed direction. The index had risen a revised two-tenths of 1 per­cent in April. Home sales dropped so sharply in May — 21 percent — that a huge April gain was more than wiped away, the Government also reported. The plunge, the biggest in 13 years, came despite the fact that buyers enjoyedsome of the lowest interest rates m two decades and relatively appealing prices. In a third report, the Con

[...TRUNCATED...]

, the first in eight months, was the main soaree of upward pressure on the index. The others were higher con­tracts and orders for plant and equip­ment, a rise in building permits and an uptick in stock prices. The length of the average workweek, the 11th component, was unchanged. Just as worrisome to some ana­lysts as the leading index, whose com­ponents move ahead of the economy as a whole, is the performance of its companion, the coincident index. The coincident index, composed of indicators that tend to move at the same time as business activity, is only slightly higher, at 125.8, than it was in December — despite a four- tenths of 1 percent May gain. And it st&nds less than two. points above the level of March 1991, the month inwhich the latest recession officially ended. In other words, the economy has been going almost nowhere for some time as gauged by the items making up the coincident index — the size of nonfarm payrolls, personal incomes, industrial production and factory and trade sales. Still another indicator, the lagging index of elements that tend to move after a time lag in the direction of the coincident index, edged up one-tenth of 1 percent last month. 'A Little Nervous' The dive in new-home sales last month, to an annual rate of 571,000, was roughly twice as large as antici­pated following an April bulge that would have been almost impossible to sustain. Michael S. Carliner, an economist for the National Association of Home
TARGET ARTICLE END
